data.title: Chart Industries: End Of An Era
data.description:
Jill Evanko, CEO of Chart Industries (NYSE:GTLS) is leading the company to a merger with Flowserve (NYSE:FLS). While the last article has dealt largely with the effects of the Howden acquisition. That is now in the past. The big deal here is that someone like Jill Evanko, CEO (President and a Director also) had a record of value creation that is going to be darn hard to keep going. Such people are generally a rarity to begin with. She will become Chairman of the Board in the newly combined company. The combined company President and CEO, Scott Rowe, will have quite a challenge to keep that record of value creation going.

As the transaction shows, this will be the end of Chart as it is currently composed. The merger with Flowserve will create a new entity going forward. At some point this likely should have been expected because the group in charge of Chart was not getting any younger and the company had to consider a succession strategy. This appears to be that succession strategy.

This is probably the first time that Chart Industry investors have heard anything about investment grade. Chart, for a long time was likely too small and too cyclical to talk about investment grade.

It did not help that all the acquisitions and mergers kept materially affecting the company prospects. Now, the company is evidently going to be large enough and diversified enough that investment grade will become a realistic goal of this merger.

This merger continues a long-held goal of management to diversify away from the dependency of large (multiyear) natural gas projects. Long-time investors may well remember that back around 2015 I covered a period where orders actually dropped to zero. That has galvanized management to "steady the ship" so that the dependency on that one area did not lead to such huge order rate swings.

As a result, Chart is now a very different company thanks to what management has accomplished. Further diversification will happen thanks to this merger that should steady earnings more while keeping the company involved with growth markets. The result is a growth company rather than a cyclical company that depended largely upon the natural gas cycle for large natural gas projects. This merger will continue that long-term journey.

Note that the aftermarket business is growing as a percentage of the business. This is likely the most countercyclical part of the business. The market likes smooth earnings. The growth of countercyclical business gives the company a steadiness that the market can easily understand.

The key to most Chart acquisitions and combinations is not the synergies savings (even though that is important). Instead, it is much more about the sales opportunities available to the combined company that were not available to the companies as separate entities. Usually, those opportunities far exceed any synergistic savings. In the past, those sales opportunities were a large component of the fast growth record of Chart. They will likely be a component of the growth record going forward.

What will likely follow the merger is a review of the sales opportunities for the combined company. Chart has generally combined the sales effort while keeping the manufacturing effort separate (and largely decentralized). Each of those product lines likely represents a past standalone company that is still free to pursue its own sales while at some level the combined effort results in more sales companywide.

As was the case in the past, post-merger company presentations will likely discuss "wins" of the combined sales effort in a communication strategy by management to assure the market of the benefits of the merger.

Similarly, the post-combination company will likely have a stock that responds to order intake (heavily) as is the case now.

Keeping each manufacturing location small makes quality control much easier to manage. It is actually a decentralized type of situation that is very similar to the Berkshire Hathaway (BRK.B)(BRK.A) whereupon each "company" largely runs the show at the respective locations. This also makes rapid growth a less risky plan.

Shown above, is a couple of the potential examples of sales opportunities of the combined market. Keep in mind that Chart alone, has some fantastic growth opportunities to some relatively large markets that have enabled the growth record of the past.

What is shown above is actually some very traditional markets long-served likely by both companies separately. Chart has long strived to be a "one-stop shop". This merger moves the post-combined company closer to that reality for a larger number of customers.

Summary

I have followed Chart for decades ever since it was a $40 million dollar company. The company name is about to be retired and replaced by an entirely new name post combination. With one exception to its illustrious history, it has largely been a bonanza for investors throughout its existence.

Since Jill Evanko is going to move to the Chairman Of The Board, this is likely to lead to a smooth transition to relatively new management of the company that should benefit current shareholders. Jill Evanko is likely a wealth of information that should get the newly combined company off to a good start.

However, going forward, Scott Rowe will be the new CEO. He has some very big shoes to fill leading the combined company. Given that the transition is likely to be smooth the way things are, this is likely to remain a strong buy consideration for at least the near future.

The market may show some stock price hesitation until there is some assurance that the combined company will do as well as Chart has done on its own. At some point, Scott Rowe will be largely "on his own" to make his own track record. I hope that record is as good as what preceded him in the form of Chart management.

Risks

Jill Evanko, CEO and President, has a record of profitable growth at Chart that few at any management level can match. Replacing her is going to prove to be a challenge. We are going to find out (probably quickly) if Scott Rowe is up to the job.

The other consideration is that as a company grows like Chart has, the rate of growth inevitably slows. Already there is talk of a dividend along the lines of what Flowserve has done in the past. This likely means a hopefully smooth transition to an income and growth idea that frequently happens as companies get larger. But there is no assurance that the whole execution happens as I laid out.

The current political uncertainty is likely one motivating factor behind this transition. I think that the current situation is really negotiations that will largely replace the current uncertainty with something permanent and constructive. However, there is no assurance that will happen.

data.title: Alphabet: Current Valuation Is Lower Than March 2020
data.description:
Introduction

I have recommended buying the YTD dip in my previous Alphabet/Google (NASDAQ:GOOGL) (NASDAQ:GOOG) (TSX:GOOG:CA) article and my take remains the same despite the stock's underperformance since March 8. Google's leadership across a few large industries is undisputed, which is combined with being among the largest players in other thriving domains. It is difficult to believe that such a wide-moat FCF monster's P/E ratio is currently at just 20, expected to move closer to just 10 over the next few years. Therefore, the rating remains the same, 'Strong buy.'

Fundamental analysis

The biggest reason why the stock market is cautious about GOOGL is that there is intensifying competition from generative AI tools in the search engine market. I agree that such a risk exists, which I explain in more detail in the 'Mitigating factors' section of the article. However, this risk is extremely remote, and we should not forget that there are two robust indications that Google's dominance in search and digital advertising will remain intact in the foreseeable future.

Google Search continues delivering robust revenue growth every quarter. In addition, I want to remind you that Google's advertising revenue is generated not only via search engines but through YouTube as well. This asset's potential to drive digital advertising growth should not be underestimated because it boasts by far the largest market share among video streaming platforms.

The global video streaming market is poised to thrive over the next several years with a very aggressive CAGR. Optimistic growth forecasts look fair because younger generations prefer streaming platforms over linear television, according to a report from PwC. As traditional television is expected to experience a secular decline, it is reasonable to expect that the gap will be filled by video streaming offerings. With YouTube's market share way ahead of the second-place Netflix, Google is extremely well positioned to capitalize on this secular trend.

We also do not see a large discrepancy between Google's digital advertising revenue growth and the full-year 2025 estimated growth for the entire digital advertising industry. According to the first table presented in the 'Fundamental analysis', Google's core business's revenue grew by 9.8% year-over-year. This is more or less in line with Statista's forecast for the global digital advertising growth of 10.7% for this year. This indicates that Google's market share remains approximately the same in the global digital advertising industry.

Developments regarding the robotaxi market also look favorable for Google. The company's Waymo continues gaining traction as it plans to expand its geographical presence further. The robotaxi service is reportedly expected to launch in Atlanta and Orlando soon. Waymo is already present in a few large cities in the U.S., which makes its way ahead of Tesla in this market. To remind readers, Tesla is still yet to launch its robotaxi service, with a tentative start date of June 12. Having this strategic advantage over Tesla is a formidable asset for Google because the industry has exponential growth potential.

Google also performs extremely well in the cloud industry. Google Cloud is the third-largest player globally by market share, trailing AWS and Microsoft Azure. Moreover, Google demonstrates a strong commitment to close the gap with leaders because its cloud revenue demonstrated much more aggressive growth compared to competitors.

Aggressive innovation is the key to outperforming competitors in such a highly technologically sophisticated industry like the cloud. From this point of view, Google is positioned much better than its closest rivals because it has by far the largest net cash position among the three giants. Therefore, Google also has great potential to expand its market share in the cloud.

Valuation analysis

The forward P/E ratio trajectory looks extremely bullish. For a fundamentally strong, wide-moat company like Google, the current P/E of around 21 is already extremely low. In addition, the P/E ratio is expected to shrink dramatically over the next few years. The FY2028 estimated forward P/E ratio of 12.4 indicates an extremely cheap valuation.

Now, the DCF it is. I use the same 7.5% discount rate as in my previous analysis because there were no shifts in the Fed's monetary policy. I also maintain the same modest 4% constant growth rate for the terminal value (TV) calculation because of Google's rock-solid fundamentals.

Wall Street top-line forecasts for FY2026-2027 are represented by more than 30 reputable investment bankers, which makes it reliable enough to use as the DCF model's assumptions. After FY2027, I applied a steady revenue growth rate decline. Google's current FCF margin is 16.87%, which is the TTM performance. The AI race will certainly require Google to invest in CapEx aggressively over the next few years. Therefore, I am conservative with the FCF margin expansion trajectory and use the same FCF margin for the entire DCF horizon. Seeking Alpha suggests that the current outstanding share count is 12.13 billion.

With all these relatively conservative assumptions, Google's fair share price is around $208, meaning there is a 24% upside. This is a massive discount for a wide-moat technological leader like GOOGL. My confidence in this DCF model is also reinforced by the fact that my fair value estimate is only slightly higher than the average $199 target price from Wall Street.

The chart below says it all. Google's TTM P/E ratio is below 20, which already sounds unrealistic. I also want to emphasize that even during the COVID-19 panic in March 2020, Google's P/E ratio fell just slightly below 24. Thus, Google is currently valued much cheaper compared to the period of vast uncertainty triggered by an unprecedented pandemic which we had never seen before.

Mitigating factors

With the emergence of generative AI offerings that are capable of searching the Internet, competition risks significantly increased for Google. While the company has been dominating the search business over decades, its generative AI offering cannot boast the same dominance. Google's Gemini is a notable player in the generative AI field, but the competition is fierce there. Apart from the most widely known ChatGPT, there are various other emerging players backed by other Magnificent 7 companies like Grok, Llama, or Anthropic Claude. There are also thriving players that might be not that well-known but also accumulate their growth momentum. For example, Perplexity's valuation has grown by multiple times over the last few months and is currently worth $14 billion.

Apart from emerging generative AI players, some well-known large companies are also expanding their footprints in digital advertising. For example, Amazon's digital advertising unit is also demonstrating strong growth momentum, with a 17.7% year-over-year revenue growth in Q1 2025. Such a growth pace is very impressive, especially taking into account that this business' scale is already quite large with quarterly revenue far above $10 billion.

Conclusion

Despite potential competition risks from emerging generative AI players, I believe that Google's prospects for the next decade are intact. The company's business mix is stellar and the management's commitment to innovate and compete is rock-solid.

data.title: Golub Capital BDC's NAV, Valuation, And Dividend Versus 11 BDC Peers - Part 1 (Includes Recommendations As Of 5/30/2025)
data.description:
Focus of Article:

The focus of PART 1 of this article is to analyze Golub Capital BDC Inc.’s (NASDAQ:GBDC) recent results and compare a handful of the company’s metrics to 11 business development company (BDC) peers. This analysis will show past and current data with supporting documentation. Table 1 will compare GBDC’s recent net asset value (NAV) economic return (loss), adjusted net investment income (NII), stock price to annualized NII ratio, and percentage of total investment income attributable to capitalized payment-in-kind (PIK)/deferred interest income to the 11 BDC peers. Table 1 will also provide a premium (discount) to estimated CURRENT NAV analysis using stock prices as of 5/30/2025. Table 2 will compare GBDC’s investment portfolio (including several additional metrics) as of 12/31/2024 and 3/31/2025 to the 11 BDC peers.

I am writing this two-part article due to the continued requests that such an analysis be specifically performed on GBDC and some of the company’s BDC peers at periodic intervals. These BDC peers include Ares Capital Corp. (ARCC), Blackstone Secured Lending Fund (BXSL), Capital Southwest Corp. (CSWC), FS KKR Capital Corp. (FSK), Gladstone Investment Corp. (GAIN), Main Street Capital Corp. (MAIN), Oaktree Specialty Lending Corp. (OCSL), Blue Owl Capital Corp. (OBDC), SLR Investment Corp. (SLRC), TriplePoint Venture Growth BDC Corp. (TPVG), and Sixth Street Specialty Lending Corp. (TSLX). Directly due to low subscriber demand/popularity, I decided to drop coverage of MidCap Financial Investment Corp. (MFIC), PennantPark Floating Rate Capital Ltd. (PFLT), and Blackrock (BLK) TCP Capital Corp. (TCPC) in January 2025.

Understanding the characteristics of a company’s investment portfolio and operating performance can shed some light on which companies are overvalued or undervalued, strictly per a “numbers” analysis. This is not the only data that should be examined to initiate a position within a particular stock/sector. However, I believe this analysis would be a good “starting point” to begin a discussion on the topic. My Buy, Sell, or Hold recommendation and current price target for GBDC will be in the “Conclusions Drawn” section of the article. This includes providing a list of the BDC stocks I currently believe are undervalued (a buy recommendation), overvalued (a sell recommendation), or appropriately valued (a hold recommendation).

NAV, Economic Return (Loss), Current Premium (Discount) to NAV, and NII Analysis – Overview:

Let us start this analysis by getting accustomed to the information provided in Table 1 below. This will be beneficial when explaining how GBDC compares to the company’s 11 BDC peers regarding the metrics stated above. Due to the fact several BDC peers listed in Table 1 have a different fiscal year-end, all quarterly results are based on a calendar year-end. For instance, all metrics below are stated “Q1 2025” even though this does not correspond to every company’s fiscal year-end. Readers should be aware of this when the analysis is presented below.

Table 1a + 1b – NAV, Economic Return (Loss), Current Premium (Discount) to NAV, NII, and Capitalized PIK Analysis

(Source: Table created by me, obtaining historical stock prices from NASDAQ and each company’s NAV per share figures from the SEC’s EDGAR Database.)

Table 1 above provides the following information on GBDC and the 11 BDC peers (see each corresponding column): 1) NAV per share at the end of calendar Q4 2024; 2) NAV per share at the end of calendar Q1 2025; 3) NAV per share change during calendar Q1 2025 (percentage); 4) economic return (loss) (change in NAV and accrued dividend) during calendar Q1 2025 (percentage); 5) economic return (loss) during the trailing 12-months (percentage); 6) my estimated CURRENT NAV per share (NAV as of 5/30/2025); 7) stock price as of 5/30/2025; 8) 5/30/2025 premium (discount) to my estimated CURRENT NAV (percentage); 9) NII (or adjusted NII where applicable) per share during calendar Q1 2025; 10) NII (or adjusted NII where applicable) per share change versus the prior quarter; 11) NII (or adjusted NII where applicable) per share change versus calendar Q1 2024; 12) 5/30/2025 stock price to annualized NII ratio; and 13) percentage of total investment income attributable to capitalized PIK (deferred) interest income during calendar Q1 2025 (percentage).

Now that an overview has been provided, let us start the comparative analysis.

Analysis of GBDC:

Using Table 1 above as a reference, GBDC had an NAV of $15.13 per share at the end of calendar Q4 2024. GBDC had an NAV of $15.04 per share at the end of calendar Q1 2025. This calculates to a quarterly NAV decrease of ($0.09) per share or (0.59%). The majority of this NAV decrease was directly in relation to a minor net realized loss associated with 2 restructurings of previous non-accrual portfolio companies during the quarter. When including GBDC’s quarterly base dividend of $0.39 per share and no special periodic dividend, the company had an economic return (change in NAV and accrued dividends) of $0.30 per share or 1.98% for calendar Q1 2025. It should also be noted, that GBDC had a trailing 12- and 24-month economic return of 11.77% and 25.87%, respectively. Both percentages were slightly above the covered 12 BDC peer average (a positive catalyst/trend).

GBDC’s performance during the past four quarters was mainly attributable to the following three factors: 1) minor net overpayment of dividends (when including special periodic dividends) when compared to the company’s NII/adjusted NII; 2) modest net realized losses within a handful of exited/restructured portfolio companies; and 3) minor net unrealized appreciation within a majority of the company’s active investment portfolio. This is a good transition to the next topic of discussion, an analysis of GBDC’s investment portfolio (including several additional metrics) as of 12/31/2024 and 3/31/2025. To begin this analysis, Table 2 is provided below.

Tables 2a + 2b – Investment Portfolio Composition Analysis (Including Several Additional Metrics; 3/31/2025 Versus 12/31/2024)

(Source: Tables created by me, directly obtaining some figures/percentages from the SEC’s EDGAR Database [link provided below Table 1]. All remaining figures/percentages were calculated using data obtained within the SEC’s EDGAR Database.)

Using Tables 2a and 2b above as a reference, GBDC had 92% and less than 1% of the company’s investment portfolio in senior secured first- and second-lien loans as of 3/31/2025, respectively. As such, these types of loans comprised the majority of GBDC’s investment portfolio. When compared to the prior quarter, GBDC’s percentage of senior secured first- and second-lien loans remained unchanged. GBDC also had less than 1%, 0%, and 7% of the company’s investment portfolio in subordinated debt (unsecured loans), collateralized loan obligations (CLO)/credit-linked notes (CLN) (structured securitizations) + other, and equity/warrants, respectively. When compared to the prior quarter, GBDC’s percentage of subordinated debt (unsecured loans), CLO/CLN (structured securitizations) + other, and equity/warrants also remained unchanged. As such, there was not a notable shift in investment portfolio composition during calendar Q1 2025.

I would also point out, that GBDC’s proportion of second-lien loans and subordinated debt is very low when compared to the company’s 11 BDC peers. Simply put, this is generally a positive characteristic regarding potential credit risk/recoveries in a recessionary environment (consider general credit hierarchy). I believe GBDC’s below-average non-accrual percentages are direct evidence of the company’s more cautionary strategy regarding capital stack/credit hierarchy.

As of 3/31/2025, GBDC’s investment portfolio had a “fair market value (FMV) versus cost” ratio of 0.9941x. When compared to the 11 other BDC peers within this analysis, this ratio was slightly below the mean of 1.0106x (a minor negative factor/trend). When compared to a ratio of 0.9920x as of 12/31/2024, GBDC’s ratio very slightly increased during calendar Q1 2025. As noted above, this was mainly the result of partially realized losses in conjunction with 2 non-accrual restructurings, offset by minor unrealized appreciation within the remainder of GBDC’s investment portfolio. A more detailed “breakdown” of GBDC’s quarterly performance was recently provided to our Investing Group subscribers via a quarterly earnings assessment article.

GBDC had 1.2% and 0.7% of the company’s investment portfolio on “non-accrual” status as of 3/31/2025; based on its amortized cost basis and FMV, respectively. When compared to the 11 BDC peers as of 3/31/2025, GBDC’s amortized cost and FMV non-accrual percentage were slightly—modestly below the mean of 3.5% and 1.9%, respectively (a positive catalyst/trend).

Since the company’s initial public offering (IPO) in 2010, GBDC’s investment portfolio as of 3/31/2025 has generated a cumulative realized loss of ($0.44) per share (when based on a per-share count as of 3/31/2025). GBDC’s cumulative realized loss figure was slight—modestly more attractive when compared to the mean loss of ($0.82) per share (a positive catalyst/trend). I believe calculating a BDC’s cumulative realized gain (loss) per share amount provides an extremely useful metric when analyzing the long-term performance of management’s underwriting abilities, due diligence, expertise, and operational performance. This metric provides direct evidence that GBDC’s management team has, regarding a majority of instances, continued to find attractive debt/equity investments over a long period of time which, more times than not, have ultimately delivered attractive risk-adjusted returns.

As of 3/31/2025, 0.08% of GBDC’s portfolio had debt and equity investments within the oil and gas sector (based on FMV; including certain investments in the energy sector which had “oil and gas” characteristics and/or services closely linked to the sector). When compared to the 11 other BDC peers within this analysis, GBDC’s oil and gas exposure was slightly below the mean of 1.45%. When compared to the prior quarter, GBDC’s exposure to the oil and gas sector remained unchanged. Even though larger oil and gas companies benefited during most of 2022 from a net increase in commodity prices tied to inflation and the ongoing Russia/Ukraine conflict, I would point out that most sector prices have largely retraced during late 2022 – 2025. In addition, smaller/private oil and gas companies are not “out of the woods” (still high-cost outlays making breakeven prices higher). This could be a short- or long-term event, dependent upon many unknown geopolitical/macroeconomic variables. As such, I would remain a bit cautious considering the ramifications of this specific sector regarding high-yield/speculative-grade credit. I believe GBDC’s continued very low percentage attributed to oil and gas companies remains a positive catalyst/trend.

Once again, using Table 1 as a reference, GBDC reported an adjusted NII (which excludes merger-related premium amortization) of $0.388 per share during calendar Q1 2025. I prefer to track/utilize GBDC’s adjusted NII metric as opposed to NII (more indicative of net investment company taxable income (ICTI)). When comparing each company’s stock price as of 5/30/2025 to its annualized NII (or where applicable, adjusted NII), GBDC had the 7th lowest ratio at 9.80x. GBDC’s annualized adjusted NII ratio was slightly below the 12-peer ratio of 10.01x as of 5/30/2025 (a slightly positive catalyst/trend). Historically speaking, GBDC’s price-to-earnings ratio as of 5/30/2025 was attractive/low (a positive catalyst/trend).

During calendar Q1 2025, 6.88% of GBDC’s total investment income was attributable to capitalized PIK/deferred interest income. When compared to the 11 other BDC peers within this analysis, this was basically at the mean of 6.87%. I believe it is never a positive catalyst/trend when a BDC has any portion of its accrued income classified as being capitalized/deferred. Simply put, under GAAP, capitalized PIK interest/dividend income is revenue that is currently being “booked” but has not actually been received in cash yet (deferred). In a majority of cases, capitalized PIK interest income is paid in cash at the maturity of that particular loan/when a sale occurs. However, in my experience, more times than not, capitalized PIK interest income is a contractual amendment regarding a specific portfolio company that is, at the time, having operational difficulties (which increases the probability of the eventual inability to pay its loan obligations). This especially holds true when a specific debt investment had no capitalized PIK feature at the origination of a particular loan but is currently accruing 100% capitalized PIK interest income due to a loan modification/restructuring.

Simply put, in a majority of cases, it is a “slick” strategy of continuing to record accrued interest income only to write off this capitalized interest income at a later date; usually at loan maturity by classifying that “lost deferred interest” as a reduction in the debt investment’s proceeds (a realized loss) as opposed to lowering previously accrued income by the accumulated capitalized PIK balance. I am not stating this occurs all the time, but certain BDC peers tend to utilize this “phantom income” strategy regularly. In particular, Prospect Capital Corp. (PSEC) has been prone to this strategy to a greater degree over the years versus all the other BDC peers within this analysis, which was pointed out for years (and one of the reasons I dropped that BDC back in 2024). As such, it could be the case that capitalized PIK interest income is never “completely” received in cash upon maturity/when a sale occurs. In my professional opinion, if a BDC has a large/above-average portion of its investment income classified/accrued as capitalized PIK interest income, it should be seen as a potential concern regarding future performance/credit quality. In the end, one really just has to go “case-by-case” to determine the overall “viability” of a BDC actually eventually receiving this capitalized PIK/deferred interest income in the future. Something I continually monitor/track in my modeling.

As of 5/30/2025, GBDC’s stock price traded at $15.21 per share. When calculated, GBDC’s stock price was trading at a premium to my estimated CURRENT NAV (NAV as of 5/30/2025; $14.95 per share) of $0.26 per share, or 1.74%. This was modestly more attractive than the 12-BDC covered peer average of a premium of 10.89% (a positive catalyst/trend). I continue to believe GBDC should trade at a minor—modest premium to the company’s CURRENT NAV. As such, based on my proven valuation methodology over various interest rate/economic cycles, I currently believe GBDC is one of a handful of BDC peers that is deemed to be appropriately valued.

Comparison of GBDC’s NAV, Economic Return, Valuation, NII, and Other Metrics to 11 BDC Peers in Ranking Order:

The REIT Forum Feature

Conclusions Drawn (PART 1):

PART 1 of this article has analyzed GBDC and 11 other BDC peers in regard to the following metrics: 1) trailing 12-month economic return (loss) (a good indicator of recent overall performance); 2) a percentage of investments on non-accrual status as of 3/31/2025 (a good indicator of overall portfolio health/credit risk); 3) cumulative gain (loss) per share as of 3/31/2025 (a great indicator of long-term performance); 4) current premium (discount) to my estimated CURRENT NAV per share (NAV as of 5/30/2025) (a very good indicator of overall valuation); 5) current stock price to annualized NII ratio (a good indicator of overall valuation); and 6) a percentage of total investment income attributable to capitalized PIK (deferred) interest income (a good indicator of overall portfolio health/credit risk).

When compared to the 11 other BDC peers within this analysis, I believe GBDC continues to outperform a majority of the company’s BDC peers I currently cover. This includes but is not limited to, GBDC’s attractive adjusted NII per share (though, to remain unbiased, has net decreased over the prior several quarters [similar to most sector peers]), a modestly below average percentage of investments on non-accrual status as of 3/31/2025, a slightly—modestly more attractive cumulative realized gain (loss) per share amount as of 3/31/2025, a very low exposure to the oil and gas sector (including certain investments in the energy sector which had oil and gas characteristics and/or services closely linked to the sector), an above average trailing 12-month economic return percentage, and a very slightly more attractive stock price to annualized adjusted NII ratio (all positive catalysts/trends).

In addition, GBDC fairly recently (effective July 2023) lowered management fees from 1.375% of total assets less cash and cash equivalents to just 1.00% which is currently tied for the lowest percentage out of the externally managed covered BDC peers. Furthermore, effective January 2024, GBDC lowered the cap/max regarding the company’s income incentive fees from 20.0% of pre-incentive fee NII to 15.0% which is currently the lowest percentage out of the externally managed covered BDC peers. This includes continuing to have the highest income incentive fee “hurdle” rate (rate of return) of 8.00%. Simply put, this will continue to positively impact shareholders via lower proportionate fees versus all other externally managed BDC covered peers.

That said, to remain non-biased, this article also highlighted that GBDC had a near average FMV versus cost ratio and capitalized PIK interest income percentage (both neutral/slightly negative factors/trends). In my opinion, GBDC’s PIK interest income percentage continues to be the company’s biggest negative factor/trend (although this percentage has been relatively stable over the past 2 years [unlike a majority of covered BDC peers]).

Looking back, I previously correctly identified the high—very high probability of multiple GBDC base dividend increases and/or special periodic dividends during late 2022–2024. This was mainly due to the U.S. London Interbank Offered Rate (LIBOR)/Standard Overnight Financing Rate (SOFR)/PRIME very quickly moving past all floors on the asset side of the balance sheet, along with refinancing outstanding borrowings with longer-term, lower-cost debt on the liability side of the balance sheet, during 2021.

That said, to remain non-biased, during 2024–2025 one had/has to continue to monitor the weighted average annualized yield on new loan originations versus existing debt investments as there remains a pretty good “lag” in this specific metric. Something I continually track. In addition, I believe the majority of sector earnings peaked during late 2023 – early 2024 (recently plateaued). The rapid NII growth that sector peers experienced during 2022–2023 simply did not occur during 2024 (nor will occur during 2025). As the FOMC began to reduce the Federal Funds Rate (started in September 2024), sector earnings will GRADUALLY decrease over time. The severity of decreases will vary peer-to-peer (which I continuously project/model). This notion is already embedded in all price targets. Furthermore, credit risk will almost certainly continue to rise (albeit mildly) throughout the sector during 2025 – early 2026 but should begin to slowly ease later in 2026.

However, I continue to believe GBDC is better positioned to weather a potential minor—modest recession when compared to most sector peers (as highlighted in the analysis above). In addition, GBDC’s “cushion” to maintain the company’s previously increased base quarterly dividend per-share rate of $0.39 per share is fairly good. That said, as correctly pointed out 2 quarters ago, GBDC’s special periodic dividend would very likely net decrease over time (similar to most sector peers).

My BUY, SELL, or HOLD Recommendation:

From the analysis provided above, including additional factors not discussed within this article (additional metrics covered in PART 2), I currently rate GBDC as a SELL when I believe the company’s stock price is trading at or greater than a 7.5% premium to my projected CURRENT NAV (NAV as of 5/30/2025; $14.95 per share), a HOLD when trading at less than a 7.5% premium but less than a (2.5%) discount to my projected CURRENT NAV, and a BUY when trading at or greater than a (2.5%) discount to my projected CURRENT NAV.

Therefore, with a closing price as of 6/3/2025 of $15.02 per share, I currently rate GBDC as APPROPRIATELY VALUED from a stock price perspective.

data.title: Meta's Strategic Nuclear Leap Bolsters The Bull Case
data.description:
Since my last Meta Platforms, Inc. (NASDAQ:META) analysis, the stock has gained 20% in price.

The new 20-year contract between Meta and Constellation Energy (CEG) for nuclear energy could not come at a better time. As energy demands increase and grid systems become fragile, Meta is leading the charge of Big Tech energy transformation to bolster American security. The U.S. electricity demand is expected to rise significantly as customers rapidly adopt data-intensive AI and robotics operations that require more power. This changing demand requires consideration of volatile pricing and grid instability due to intermittent renewable sources. Natural gas pricing is one example of this risk, as prices (NG1:COM) went from below $3/MMBtu (Million British Thermal Units) to over $9/MMBtu between 2021 and 2022 due to international crises and supply chain issues.

Meta's recent contract is a pivot towards energy security, providing fixed pricing of likely about $80/MWh (megawatt-hour) of carbon-free electricity, which is very favorable considering current market averages of $50/MWh or more. The average market price for renewable energy per megawatt-hour will almost certainly rise substantially as the autonomous revolution picks up steam. High demand for AI and robotics will likely lead to increasing energy prices amid a gold rush for energy providers who can acclimatize quickly. $80 might not be dirt-cheap now, but it certainly is on track to be in five to 10 years amid advanced tech proliferation at scale. Unlike many other renewable energy agreements, the Meta-Constellation nuclear agreement is reliable and supportive of strong financial management, as it crucially provides baseload power that doesn’t vary based on weather.

Meta's Power Purchase Agreement Fortifies Its Position In The AI Revolution

By entering into an agreement with Constellation, Meta ensured that it will receive 1,121 MW per year from the Clinton Nuclear Station, starting in 2027. The agreement would effectively hedge against the volatility of energy price fluctuations and provide predictable operating costs and budget stability for the company in long-term planning. Meta has lock-in pricing for energy of likely about $80/MWh (though it could be lower), which looks very good in comparison to their competitors, with Microsoft's Three Mile Island restart expected to cost around $110/MWh.

If the wholesale price of electricity goes to $100/MWh, based on the Meta-Constellation contract, Meta could save around $176 million in annual pricing (at 90% uptime—$196 million at 100% uptime), enhancing operating margins substantially. Should prices increase moderately to approximately $80/MWh, the contract achieves cost neutrality and delivers important budgeting predictability. In a downside case where market prices stabilize at $60/MWh in the long term, Meta would be responsible for a modest premium of $176 million on an annual basis (i.e., the price of long-term cost certainty). This coverage and limited downside exposure highlights the strategic rationale of agreeing to lock in stable and carbon-free electrical generation for long-term, secure AI access on Meta’s platforms.

Healthy Margins & Durable Growth

Meta's rapidly growing energy footprint (14,975 GWh [gigawatt-hours]) of energy in 2023 that could grow considerably through 2030 due to AI/data center build-outs) elevates the strategic rationale for fixed-cost procurement. Meta's expected annual energy costs will probably be over $1 billion and would very likely be more volatile without hedging.

By 2030, Meta's Clinton purchase power agreement will provide a degree of cost stability over approximately 10% of its expected total power needs, contributing substantially to margin predictability. Assuming volatility, fixed pricing allows Meta to accumulate savings over 10 years of $1 billion or greater in a base case, which should contribute 20-30 basis points annually to EBITDA margins. In isolation this may seem modest, but when viewed in aggregate it forms a powerful and important layer of earnings predictability and a reliability to free cash flow that underpins a stronger investment case. In other words, Meta can’t be the world’s top AI social media company without reliable energy to sustain it. Energy is the soul of the autonomous revolution.

Widespread Positive Multipolar Sentiment Drives Valuation Upside

Meta's proactive approach to energy risk management warrants expanding valuation multiples as market sentiment compounds positively. Meta's trailing 12-month normalized price-to-earnings ratio is around 25x, indicating the market’s enthusiasm for the company’s long-term earnings growth. The addition of reduced volatility and enhanced ESG (environmental, social, and governance) positioning is one foundational element, among many, that justifies the price-to-earnings ratio expanding toward 30x. A price target of around $800 within 12 months is easily achievable, based on normalized trailing 12-month earnings per share of $27 in the middle of 2026.

Tesla may look a little expensive compared to its moving averages, but this doesn't really concern me due to the long-term growth thesis, shrewd management team, and AI optionality that is almost unquantifiable at this stage. I'm stoutly bullish, and I think we should expect Meta to continue to trade along and above its 50-week moving average indefinitely during the AI and robotics era. Moments of growth stagnation will likely be supported by shareholder-friendly actions like buybacks and dividend increases, keeping sentiment elevated.

I believe CEO Mark Zuckerberg should be commended for his razor-focused dedication to bipartisan social media success across Western-alliance nations. This has helped to sustainably compound the company's returns recently amid varying political conditions. Everybody's role is different!

Meta’s Nuclear Initiative Shows Bold & Visionary Commitment

Compared to its peer tech companies—Microsoft (MSFT), Google (GOOGL, GOOG), and Amazon (AMZN)—Meta’s deal introduces unique and immediate operational benefits that lead the way in terms of reliability, cost of a new energy source, and climate leadership. Google's and Amazon’s nuclear strategies consist of advanced reactors and small modular reactors, with projects that will not even be operational until the 2030s. Microsoft’s partnership to restart Three Mile Island comes with substantial upfront costs and technical uncertainty. Meta has a uniquely advantageous market position in terms of well-timed, relatively low-cost, and secure nuclear energy.

With operational nuclear energy beginning in 2027, Meta will realize a first-mover advantage in its marketplace, delivering a continuous and reliable power supply crucial for scaling AI computing workloads. Meta has a competitive moat, and energy strategies like this are the foundation of the fortress. By the time 2027 arrives, Meta can couple firm, clean, baseload capacity before their competitors can, which makes its shareholders less vulnerable to price and reliability risk.

Navigating Uncertainties Is Part Of The Journey

Meta's nuclear road map is not without risks, but these are largely surmountable and supported by strong macroeconomic tailwinds regarding consensus support for energy security amid massive AI demand and geopolitical uncertainties.

Regulatory risk is also prominent. The long-term viability of Meta's nuclear positioning—and its cost-effectiveness—will depend upon the continued pro-nuclear macro context and the timely relicensing of the Clinton plant when its current agreement expires. Even though the political environment is friendly toward nuclear today, political opposition to the energy form from high-intensity ESG proponents could cause road blocks. This would most likely be due to people focusing on the nuclear energy risk and waste level compared to generally safer forms of generation like solar. This could pose demand constraints for nuclear power and lower its cost, leading to Meta's fixed-price nuclear strategy underperforming.

There is a geopolitical risk as well; while it is unlikely, a disruption in the global uranium supply chain could present operational challenges. Meta can minimize that possibility by helping to add more domestic enrichment capacity and working with other fuel sources. Operationally, unplanned outages at Clinton could cause Meta to go out to the market and purchase replacement power (probably at a premium). That said, an agreement that allows for an alternative clean energy credit or compensation would mitigate some of the potential burden. In looking to diversify via additional nuclear and renewable projects with strong contracts, Meta is well positioned to deal with any operational uncertainty.

I want to keep it clear in the front of your mind that Meta's nuclear contract enhances its investment attractiveness by giving it a rare type of resiliency against both macroeconomic and operational risk. Predictable energy costs reduce earnings volatility and improve the steady reliability and cash flow that Meta can return to its investors during volatile times. This increased stability provides justification for a lower discount rate (it creates less risk inherently), which contributes to overall increased intrinsic value. Second, the contract gives Meta a first-mover advantage within a bleeding-edge trend in zero-carbon, sustainable, long-term, and reliable energy sourcing from nuclear. Although nuclear is contentious in some ESG circles, this will sway many ESG-mandated funds and investors to be bullish on where Meta is headed.

Conclusion: Strong Buy

Meta Platforms' nuclear deal with Constellation is not a traditional energy procurement agreement, but rather a strategy that leaned into foresight and elite corporate stewardship with agile and margin-sensitive execution. The deal synched energy resiliency with operational predictability and ESG leadership into a cohesive strategy around the company's core business of long-term AI-empowered social media dominance. It’s a deal that was born out of visualized looming weakness that was channeled into present strength. This is yet another reason that makes bullish valuations of Meta both valid and worthy. This is truly the inkling of an emerging narrative—a technology behemoth on a defined path to proactively reshape its risk profile through private-access clean energy infrastructure. If the strategy evolves more abundantly over time, Meta will not just be resilient to macroeconomic volatility, but it will thrive as the dominant global AI social media empire for decades to come.

data.title: Palantir: When The Bear Case Breaks, It Breaks Fast
data.description:
What if the most misunderstood stock in the AI revolution wasn’t a chipmaker or a model builder, but a protocol company hiding in plain sight, one that builds decision infrastructure, not just software? Palantir Technologies (NASDAQ:PLTR) has quietly become the operating system for institutional intelligence across government and commercial sectors, yet most of Wall Street still misprices it using SaaS yardsticks.

While legacy analysts debate whether its 200x P/E is justifiable, they’re missing the forest for the trees. In my view, Palantir is no longer just selling tools; it’s embedding itself as the substrate for mission-critical execution. In Q1 2025, Palantir didn’t just beat expectations, it redefined what software can do when it becomes inseparable from institutional memory, logistics, and decisions. Palantir is now up 42% since my bullish call, yet the market still misunderstands its true trajectory.

With a Rule of 40 score of 83%, commercial contract values up 183% YoY, and adjusted free cash flow margins exceeding 40%, Palantir now outperforms not just on story, but on substance. This is no longer a speculative play. It's a monetization engine compounding inside multi-decade contracts, backed by battlefield-tested technology and organizational lock-in.

For those still using P/S multiples to judge Palantir, the train has already left the station. We’re not just early. We’re calibrated for where this is going, and we’re not selling.

The “Overvaluation” Fallacy, Palantir Is Priced for What It’s Already Becoming

The prevailing bear argument, that Palantir’s price implies perfection, needs to be updated. Such an argument does not take into consideration the shift from multiple expansion narratives to concrete, compounding monetization. Palantir’s high forward P/S (nearly 80x) and forward P/E (over 200) are, according to skeptics, proof of euphoria. These surface-level metrics fail to consider deeper capital cycle dynamics. Palantir is not just selling software licenses or chasing transitory AI hype; it’s systematically converting pilot programs into long-term infrastructure contracts worth hundreds of millions over their lifetimes.

In the first quarter of 2025, U.S. commercial revenue rose by 71% YoY and 19% quarter over quarter to exceed a $1 billion annualized run rate, metrics which point to sustainable second-order growth momentum and not to cyclical spikes. Crucially, it was delivered on an adjusted operating margin of 44% and $370 million in free cash flow, affording the business a Rule of 40 score of 83%, a rate reached by just a handful of top software companies.

In addition, critics who claim PLTR is "too big to grow fast" overlook the unusually high LTV/CAC dynamics in its flywheel. Total contract value in U.S. commercial grew 183% YoY to $810 million, and deal value growth remained steady at 127% YoY to $2.3 billion. These aren't projections, but signed deals. The Acquire–Expand–Scale model in the company does not pursue vanity metrics or waste capital to acquire growth; it fosters long gestation periods that compound into deep penetration into accounts.

That lag time between TCV and GAAP revenue is not a weakness, but an embedded call option on delayed yet certain revenue. In contrast to incumbent SaaS businesses subject to seat-count limits, Palantir scales in customers. The automation returns from AIP agents form a flywheel where not just every deployment decreases operating expense but yields more use-case specific data, leading to more agents, more automations, and more monetization.

Bears often compare Palantir to Microsoft or Salesforce, which don't have such second-order monetization loops. So, the "overvaluation" is not about pricing perfection but about infrastructural inevitability. The long-duration visibility into U.S. commercial revenue, underlying productivity flywheels, and higher conversion of free cash flows imply the price is more than likely underestimating the transition from software to AI-native protocols.

Since the initial bullish call on PLTR the stock has moved up by 42%, trading at $132, moving closer to the earlier intrinsic value estimate of $175/share. Bears claim it’s “priced for perfection,” but this premise fails under examination. I believe Palantir is no ordinary SaaS company. It’s emerging as a protocol infrastructure for institutional decision-making. Measuring it using EV/Sales or P/E is analogous to valuing an airport based on the number of gates.

Execution Lag Is a Moat, Not a Risk, The Power of Controlled Compounding

Another commonly held bear thesis is that Palantir's growth slows due to friction in its onboarding model: pilots are time-intensive, integration is customized, and revenue is recognized subsequent to deal signatures. This critique is correct in direction, but it fundamentally misses the compounding effect of high-friction enterprise software.

What appears to be friction is, in fact, deliberate: the slowness in Palantir’s model is a moat cleverly disguised as a bottleneck. In Q1 2025, Palantir closed on 139 deals over $1 million and 31 over $10 million, an all-time high for the company. These are not rapid-cycle API integrations; they are full-stack AI agent installations that become embedded in mission-critical processes in finance, defense, logistics, and healthcare.

Time to execute is not a drag; it is an extended gestation period for deeply interconnected software that can't be simply replaced. AIP installations in AIG and Cardinal Health are not automating processes; they are rewriting whole operating cadences through agents focused on productivity gains of 50x, with 50% overtime savings.

Bears claim Palantir's growth relies too heavily on U.S. growth, with subdued international expansion. True, 71% of Q1 revenue was U.S.-derived, and international traction was slower. That is not a weakness, it is by design. U.S. commercial TAM is underpenetrated, and the government space provides unparalleled credibility and cash flow stability. Instead of splintering its onboarding and support capability in pursuit of international logos, Palantir is doubling down on depth, not breadth. This targeted growth not only preserves gross margins but provides rich blueprints for U.S.-based deployments that, over time, lower onboarding costs overseas.

In addition, Palantir's software is not simply sticky, it is parasitic. Once you install it, it translates enterprise information into its form and creates automation workflows through AIP agents that continue to learn. In contrast to developer-centric solutions such as Snowflake, Palantir's AI does not have to be retrained or reconfigured by end-users. Instead, it evolves across the enterprise, essentially becoming institutional knowledge. The more time it spends deployed, the more difficult it is to replace.

Even macro threats like Pentagon spending reductions and geopolitical trade barriers are overblown in the bear thesis. U.S. government revenue was up 45% YoY in Q1, and Palantir keeps winning multi-year defense contracts and renewals. In the meantime, European retrenchment out of U.S. defense procurement has little effect due to Palantir’s focus on allied defense systems (i.e., NATO, Five Eyes).

Global underperformance is an existing unknown, not an unexpected disruption threat. In the end, Palantir's "slow" sales cycle is not a weakness, it is a vendor lock-in conversion funnel. Unseen by many, unlike commoditized cloud AI players or SaaS point solutions, Palantir gets paid for certainty, not velocity. By creating systems that are difficult to rip out and replace, Palantir prioritizes permanence over viral growth, and that is precisely what infrastructure investors ought to desire.

From Defence to Deterrence: The AI Stack for National Power

The market still refers to Palantir as a defense contractor disguised as a software company. This does the company an injustice, however. It underestimates the role of AI in contemporary deterrence and the primacy that Palantir has in facilitating that deterrence. Maven Smart System, TITAN vehicles, and Warp Speed for reindustrialization are not specialty efforts; they are components in a national stack of AI. And Palantir resides at the center.

These results in Q1 2025 bear testament to this. U.S. government revenue accelerated 45% YoY and represents 42% of revenue. 32 member nations signed on to use Palantir’s AI-based mission command solution. Maven saw its usage double in half a year. TITAN, the prime contractor for Palantir, is on time and on budget and is revolutionizing the integration of hardware and software.

Government software is plagued by bloat, shelfware, and a lack of transparent ROI. In my view, Palantir flips that dynamic. Contracts expand because of observable performance metrics, target identification, logistics optimization, and parsing real-time ISR data. It builds a cultural moat: under budget cuts and scrutiny (like DOGE programs), only the best tools remain. Palantir does well under a microscope because it was designed for combat, literally and in the marketplace.

Though the European market falls behind in adoption, overseas U.S. allies are stepping up. Palantir's government work is structurally sound, performance-proven, and geopolitically attuned. It also acts as an upstream validator for commercial customers: if your military has enough faith in Palantir to grant it battlefield autonomy, why wouldn't your bank have the same level of confidence in supply chains or underwriting?

Shareholder Alignment and Cash Engines: Overlooked Capital Discipline

Underlying Palantir's philosophical missives and frontier jargon is a surprisingly financially disciplined business. During Q1 2025, Palantir reported $370 million in adjusted free cash flow on $884 million in revenue, a 42% margin. GAAP net income was $214 million on a 24% margin. Unlike many growth tech companies, Palantir isn't spending to buy growth. It's growing off the base of operating leverage, not through dilution.

Adj. OpEx only grew 8% sequentially, while revenue was up 39%. SBC declined substantially to $155 million or approximately 17.5% of revenue vs. 24% a year earlier. More importantly, employer-related tax expense on equity has become highly predictable as a reflection of a mature equity compensation arrangement. The company still has more than $5.4 billion in cash and equivalents on the balance sheet that provide tremendous strategic optionality.

Palantir's incentive system for equity pays for profitability, not only for growth. It is clear in its GAAP profitability run that now spans seven quarters. Whereas peers use trick non-GAAP accounting or R&D capitalization shenanigans, Palantir's non-GAAP adjustments are primarily SBC-related. Its cash flow conversion ranks among the best in the industry.

As buybacks resume ($918 million still authorized), the company is communicating confidence in long-term value. Especially as the float dwindles and cash flows accelerate. Unlike unprofitable AI startups, Palantir has the ability to compound within, with no need for the issuance of equity. That alone warrants a premium.

Risk Analysis: Algorithmic Monoculture and the Fragility of Ontological Lock-In

Although Palantir’s deep integration into workflows, particularly through Ontology and AIP agent stacks, is unprecedented vendor lock-in, it begets a subtle yet perilous form of systemic vulnerability: algorithmic monoculture. Palantir’s unification under one architecture of decision logic, data pipelines, and automation agents is its greatest technical strength, and it is this that leads to clients increasingly making high-stakes decisions through one system.

Centralizing decisions into one leads to efficiency, but it also means the concentration of risks. In domains such as defense, healthcare, or finance, where AIP is driving autonomous decisions, a latent defect, biased training data, or model drift across one application can lead to error across an entire enterprise. In contrast to traditional SaaS tooling that operates in modules, the Palantir architecture affords compounding but also systemic failure modes. What makes this threat so acute is the lack of redundancy in institutions. Palantir’s ontology is not just software; it is the enterprise memory and logic layer.

Over time, customers may strip internal teams of their skills, over-rely on Palantir’s domain-specific solutions, and under-invest in parallel frameworks. This builds a future where customers can't readily change vendors, or even audit the models underlying their decisions. Whereas this creates revenue resilience for Palantir, it leaves investors vulnerable to the threat that one high-visibility failure, technical, ethical, or both, could induce compounding distrust and geopolitical scrutiny. In such highly coupled systems, resilience is not simply a technical problem but an existential investor concern. This is a moat, yes, but one that needs to be jealously guarded.

Conclusion

Palantir is no longer a speculative software vendor but a protocol-layer AI infrastructure firm compounding durable free cash flows. With 71% YoY U.S. commercial growth, 44% adjusted operating margins, and $370 million FCF in Q1 2025, it's embedding itself into the decision-making fabric of governments and Fortune 500s.

The so-called "overvaluation" ignores its unmatched stickiness, second-order monetization loops, and strategic alignment with U.S. national power. Execution risk exists, but it doubles as a moat. Palantir’s valuation reflects inevitability, not euphoria. Investors should focus not on trailing multiples, but on its transformation into the AI operating system of the enterprise world.

data.title: Market Pricing In Status Quo + Valuation Can Only Take You So Far
data.description:
Listen here or on the go via Apple Podcasts and Spotify

The Pragmatic Investor, James Foord, talks macro flows and dynamics in the market (0:40). Interesting international picture - has something fundamentally shifted? (6:20) Valuation can only take you so far (11:00). Robinhood, HIMS and other favorite stocks right now (13:45). Energy Transfer and picking oil players (16:15). Owning bitcoin and/or bitcoin proxies (20:40). CoreWeave, Nebius and AI (25:00). Bearish on Super Micro Computer (26:25). Still a Tesla bull (28:30).

Transcript

Rena Sherbill: James Foord, welcome back to Investing Experts. Welcome back to Seeking Alpha. You run The Pragmatic Investor.

For those who forgot, we had you on a few times in previous years. It's great to talk to you again. Welcome back to the show.

James Foord: Thank you very much, it's great to be back.

RS: It's good to have you. As a refresher, if you could share with investors, you conducted some interviews on this podcast; you were an interviewee on some episodes. I think it would be great as a refresher for listeners to share how you approach the markets, what you specifically focus on in your analysis of stocks and the broader markets.

JF: Of course. Anyone who follows Seeking Alpha regularly might be familiar with some of my work. I've been writing for Seeking Alpha for, I think, coming in on six, seven years. And my approach is, I think it's shifted a little bit since the beginning.

Definitely started being a bit more value focused. And I think with time, I've definitely leaned a little bit more into the macro analysis. I think definitely in the last few years, it's just become a a much more important piece of the equation really. But I try and kind of have a holistic approach.

I have this thing that I tentatively call the pragmatic investing pyramid, which is basically macro at the bottom, fundamentals in the middle, and just to top it off, a little bit of technical analysis.

I think everything helps. That's the point of pragmatic investors being open to anything, not dismissing anything just because of a stigma or anything like that. Try and touch on a bit of everything, but definitely more of the macro focus.

RS: I'd be interested to hear why you decided or what made you decide that macro was such an essential part of focusing on the markets and stock picking.

It seems that in this day that we're in, this time that we're in, macro seems very, very important in terms of navigating the stock market with the tariff conversations, with the geopolitical conversations, with all the changes happening so often and so quickly. How did you make that decision, and what would you say about this current moment vis a vis focusing on the macro picture a bit more than than maybe others do?

JF: For me, I think it was just after a few years in the market, kind of realizing that a lot of the flows and dynamics in the market seem to revolve around some important macro indicators. I mean, for good or for bad, we are in a world which is maybe heavily dependent, some would say, too heavily dependent, on support from central banks, international flows, and these these kind of dynamics, I think, are really shaping markets.

And I think you can definitely see it. We had reasonably volatile markets in the last few years. I mean, we had COVID where everything crashed, then we had that big kind of almost, I guess you could say that bubble going to 2021.

I think part of it is the reason why I've kind of shifted more to the macro is because it's important to understand those things. I mean, from a purely value perspective, you might have missed a lot of the a a lot of upside in recent years.

There's a lot of stocks, a lot of things that move, but not necessarily so much to do with value even though I think value is important. It might become more important in coming years.

Ultimately, I think that's shaping things more. And, you know, never has that been more true than now, with President Trump back in the White House, creating some volatile scenarios.

And, again, lot of shifts happening in terms of geopolitics even on the monetary side of things both here and abroad.

Europe also going through some interesting times. Of course, China. Basically we've seen a very fundamental shift in the market. I guess the market's interpretation of Trump and Trump's policies.

So when he came into office, there was that big rally going into that sell the news event, shortly after, we were hit with all the volatility, all the stuff about tariffs. Trump kinda came in with these big ideas threatening, quote, unquote, to put on big tariffs and also DOGE basically trying to balance the budget.

So kind of kind of very radical ideas now, right, of actually perhaps trying to balance the budget, which, some people would have said is good. But, from a macro perspective, maybe some investors thinking that's not so good in terms of wanting to cut spending and austerity.

Tariffs, of course, not so good for global trade. But, there was this idea. You know, Trump was very clear, and it seemed for a while that he was willing to deal with the pain. Willing to deal with this kind of short term term pain in order to put the US back in a path of reindustrialization and maybe catching up with the rest of the world in some of the areas where maybe it's fallen behind.

But, more recently, I think it was just last week where the term TACO Tuesday was coined, which is this idea that whenever Trump comes out and says something about tariffs, you buy that.

You buy that dip because then he'll come out on Tuesday and reverse all that.

And that's what we've seen and I think that's kind of what the market is pricing in now is that maybe either there's no political will or just the support for these kind of policies. On the one hand, DOGE, I think it's kind of fallen short of a lot of its original objectives. They were talking about cutting in the trillions and obviously, that's never gonna happen. Just because of the amount of actual spending that they can cut, it's just not really gonna happen.

We have the new spending bill that Trump's proposed, which once we dig into it and look at all the tax cuts, it's not really very austere, let's say. It is the big beautiful spending bill as he calls it.

And then on the tariffs, we're kind of seeing that lift up again. But, again, it seems like common sense, you might say, would prevail. And China's a bit of a hold out, so I think definitely we can expect some more volatility.

But I think now the market is pricing in that continuation of the status quo, so to speak.

And this is a dynamic we've seen happening for the last twenty years, which is high spending, probably with Jerome Powell leaving the Fed and Trump getting someone new in there, probably much more lenient on monetary policy and ultimately, pretty bullish backdrop generally for financial assets.

RS: Internationally, there's been a lot of talk about not over investing in the United States right now. What would you say about the international picture?

JF: Definitely very interesting. Like, we saw that outperformance in foreign markets, especially Europe. Question is, is that a short term trend?

If you look back historically, the US tends to just outperform, these things kind of balance out.

Is it the kind of trade that you wanna fade, or is it something fundamentally shifting? And what's very interesting, I do think there is some long term tailwinds.

You've seen for example in Europe especially, spending really shift up, that fiscal spending with Germany increasing their spending, other countries in the area.

Of course, you can't compare Europe and the US in terms of technology, but, I think there's some idea that maybe they can finally catch up a little bit. I think that's being represented in some of the flows that we've gotten into other countries.

Then I think emerging markets, I personally think they're very interesting, right now. China, obviously, is hard to invest in right now, but, we've actually seen a bit of a reversal.

But I think a lot of other countries, especially if you look at places in South America or even Southeast Asia, some very some very interesting dynamics happening there and especially I think in the face of what has been a very weak dollar.

So, if you believe the dollar is going to keep weakening, which I do believe to an extent, I think, obviously, it's very good to get some diversification away from the US.

Currency is a very particular subject. I don't really focus on currencies particularly, but, there's certain economies I like. I think, you know, South America.

I think if you look at Argentina has had a great turnaround. Mexico is doing very well from this kind of breakup of US and China. And there's definitely some interesting place in Southeast Asia.

RS: Do you get into country ETFs at all? Is that something that you dabble in?

JF: Yeah. Absolutely. I have a macro ETF portfolio, I call it, which is basically thematic. We focus on different areas. We did pretty well to invest in Argentina (ARGT) a while back when they had that whole, big political transition. We have some exposure to Brazil.

Mexico (EWW) recently got some exposure to Korea, which I think could also South Korea (EWY) definitely could be an interesting option. So, again, just, it's obviously a case by case, but it is interesting in the last few months.

We've definitely seen a trend of investing away from the US. Now I don't think that is necessarily gonna continue. I'm not some kind of US defeatist.

I don't think that the empire is over or anything like that. I mean, if you look at the US, they still command a very large part of the economy. I do think the US is going to continue being the reserve currency, the dollar.

And, in terms of companies, I think, obviously, it's just a completely different story when you look at actual tech company and innovation. I think the US still has amazing opportunities. But, I think the word is diversifying. It's hedging its options, and it's good to stay diversified. It's also one of my tenants.

RS: A bit of pragmatism. I would say diversification certainly an essential part of pragmatism. You mentioned Jerome Powell earlier, and there's been rumors that he's gonna resign imminently.

How are you thinking about that, navigating that? How would you encourage investors to think about that if he does resign, if he does leave office, if he doesn't resign, if he doesn't leave office so soon? What what are your thoughts there?

JF: It's hard to know. I mean, it's pretty speculative at this point, but, ultimately, I think he's due to leave next year anyway. I think so. That's ultimately the play here is you kinda have to understand that he is going to eventually leave, probably going to get a more dovish Fed chair.

And in any case, I think the Federal Reserve will be looking at cutting rates at some point in the not too distant future.

But, in terms of actually speculating on Jerome Powell leaving, I mean, he might leave. He might, it's hard to know. Part of the pragmatism as well comes from, you can't trade every headline, which I think has been proven by the recent volatility and everything that happens around Trump.

You just have to see things in the bigger picture and try not to focus so much on the day to day.

RS: You mentioned that you cover a number of different sectors. I think one of the places I would like to start in terms of assessing how you're looking at stocks and and how you decide which stocks to get into and which stocks not to get into.

Something that we've been talking about and something I think that's deserving of being talked about is the valuation conversation when it comes to some of these bigger names, I think especially in the tech sector, but not just in the tech sector.

It seems more difficult than ever to properly value some of these stocks. Palantir (PLTR) is is a specific example that I know that you cover that comes to mind. NVIDIA's (NVDA) another one.

How would you best describe how you value these stocks, especially in this moment, where there's a lot of promise, but not yet everything has been delivered?

JF: I think that's that's the challenge. And, again, that's where the pragmatism comes in because valuation can only take you so far.

If you looked at valuation and maybe stuck just to valuation, I would have missed a lot of gains on Palantir, which has been a great performer.

I wrote the other day, it's the most, arguably, the most overvalued mega cap in history. But there's ways to justify it. I made the example there. Is Palantir, for example, the next Tesla (TSLA), or is it the next Nvidia?

Because, again, these are two companies. Tesla's a great example of a company that really got ahead of itself with the whole EV thing and pretty much gone nowhere over the last five years. I don't have the chart in front of me. I mean, it's not necessarily about buy and hold, but things can get ahead of themselves.

With Palantir specifically I argue that there is, if you are an AI bull and you think that this technology can be transformative, which I certainly think is a big possibility, then Palantir, I think could be closer to an example of an early stage NVIDIA, where you started started to see revenues suddenly appreciate thirty, forty, 50 percent, something which, at the time with NVIDIA, no one saw coming.

And I think now with Palantir, it's like, well, people kind of seeing you know, they're trying to preempt this and and see this and see this coming with Palantir.

I mean, it's possible. You know, I think I have made, you know, actual valuations where, you know, you you can justify, you know, hundred and $20 on Palantir.

I think, you know, again, it takes it takes some very bullish valuations, obviously, much above analyst estimates, but, again, that those are things that you can't always price in.

Right? I mean, the app no one knew what was gonna happen with Nvidia was it was hard for them to know. And, you know, to that in that regard, yes, Palantir is objectively overvalued, but, again, you you can't always price in everything.

And, yeah, obviously, there's other things to take into account. I mean, at this point, Palantir is also kind of it's it's almost a bit of a meme stock.

So, again, you know, trades trades with the market that way. But, now you have Nvidia. I mean, to me, Nvidia at this point is, you know, I don't think NVIDIA is that overvalued really. I mean, that's what happened.

Right? It kind of grew into its valuation, and I'm pretty bullish on like I said, I'm pretty bullish on AI. You know, every time I hear Jensen speak, I I could see I I can see why NVIDIA is the market leader.

I believe they're going to maintain that leadership. And I don't think NVIDIA is that overvalued really when you actually see, what the rest of the market is is trading like.

So, you know, NVIDIA actually can be justified from a valuation perspective. Yeah. For Palantir, you need to believe a lot more in the in the growth story, but not with NVIDIA so much.

RS: Maybe share with listeners what your favorite stocks are right now and and why they're your favorite stocks.

JF: Some of my favorite stocks right now. I mean, definitely some of the the growth names right now, have done very well.

Stocks that have done very well in recent weeks, Robinhood (HOOD), of course, I think is one that I haven't written about a lot, but I did write about it. I called it the trillion dollar opportunity, with this big shift in wealth going from Gen x boomers to millennials and, this kind of being the the app of choice, stuff like Robinhood has done very well.

Hims (HIMS) is a very interesting one, which obviously has just absolutely exploded. I also have that in my portfolio. Question is, do you wanna buy these stocks right now? That's a much harder question to answer, but I think there's still a lot of growth in AI.

I still think markets are gonna keep going up. So to that extent, something like Robinhood is very good.

But, at the same time, I would say, on a more contrarian, I'm now also starting to look a little bit at oil plays, something that maybe could act as a bit more of an inflation hedge because I think that could be the next next stage now in terms of macro could be a little bit of more elevated inflation than maybe people expect and the market is expecting.

And I think that's already priced in if you look at Bitcoin (BTC-USD). Obviously, I've always talked a lot about Bitcoin. Very interesting what's happening with Bitcoin. I pointed out in my last article that we've actually seen Bitcoin appreciate as yields have gone up.

So not acting as a risk asset so much, but actually as a monetary hedge, more in line with gold. For longer term perspective, I still think that Bitcoin's very bullish. I still have MicroStrategy (MSTR). I think that's a good stock to hold long term, but also, obviously, some of the miners.

I think miners performed pretty well in Q1, don't quote me, but I think it was the best performing sector maybe in Q1. And, yeah, I think there's definitely some value there long term. And, I was looking at oil.

Just I just wrote today about Energy Transfer (ET), so I think some exposure to oil could be good as we move into what could be quite a different kind of different scenario from a macro perspective over the next few years even, which I think is gonna be maybe a lot more inflationary and to that extent, I do think growth stocks could perhaps take a little bit of a step back, so we'll see.

It could be a very pretty big shift. I still don't have quite have all the pieces together. There's a lot of things to put together here like AI, of course, which, is gonna really change things. Obviously, history doesn't repeat, but it's definitely gonna be interesting.

RS: In terms of the oil players, how do you decide which ones to get into at this moment?

JF: Relying on the on the pyramid, just looking a little bit at the valuation and, obviously, the fundamentals.

And then also technical analysis helps. I think it's important to look at how the chart is moving and just try and pick good good levels.

I mean with Energy Transfer, I think I like the idea that they don't have too much exposure to the commodity price, but, at the same time, pretty much involved in the whole infrastructure story, which again, I think is an AI story.

Something I've talked about before is that the idea that if AI is going to take off, one of the big bottlenecks is going to be energy and perhaps energy distribution.

And, that's something that Energy Transfer is already benefiting from with some of the recent deals they've made. And, they have, I think, 5,000,000,000 in capital expenditures earmarked for next year.

So kind of really showing that they believe in the growth story. And then, again, I crunch the numbers a little bit. And to me, it just looks undervalued, really, at the current price. So that's kinda what I look for.

RS: And in terms of the fundamentals, are there particular metrics that you focus on more than others?

JF: Yeah. And that's going to kind of depend a little bit on the stock. I mean, for example, if I was looking at tech and growth places, the price earnings growth is obviously always one of the most interesting ones because that's pricing in the expectations of the future.

Sometimes you can look at the PE and it looks kind of outrageous, but, again, once you account for the growth, something like price dynamics growth is very important.

With something like an income stock or something a bit more conservative value, you'd wanna look at other metrics, obviously, price to that regard, maybe something like cash flow. Obviously, price to cash flow, obviously, very important as well.

With something like Energy Transfer, I'm looking at something like price to cash flow with high growth stocks, obviously, something like the price earnings growth, if there are earnings, of course.
